title: Virtual dispatch and abstract interfaces

canonicalExplanation.summary: Virtual dispatch selects the final overrider through a base reference or pointer, and a polymorphic base intended for deletion needs a virtual destructor.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A vector of unique_ptr<Shape> calls virtual draw on each dynamic object and deletes safely through Shape because its destructor is virtual. Your goal is to implement and trace runtime dispatch through a small abstract interface. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Class interface and invariants; unique_ptr, shared_ptr and non-owning access; Public inheritance and substitution. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Virtual dispatch selects the final overrider through a base reference or pointer, and a polymorphic base intended for deletion needs a virtual destructor. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Calls during construction and destruction dispatch only within the active construction level, default arguments are statically selected, and object slicing removes derived state. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Any same-named derived function is dynamically selected even when the base function is not virtual or the object was copied by value.

includes: Define virtual operations, abstract bases and virtual destructors for polymorphic ownership.
Implement and trace runtime dispatch through a small abstract interface.
excludes: Calls during construction and destruction dispatch only within the active construction level, default arguments are statically selected, and object slicing removes derived state.

outcomes: Implement and trace runtime dispatch through a small abstract interface.

examples.title: Virtual dispatch and abstract interfaces: defined case
examples.situation: A vector of unique_ptr<Shape> calls virtual draw on each dynamic object and deletes safely through Shape because its destructor is virtual.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A vector of unique_ptr<Shape> calls virtual draw on each dynamic object and deletes safely through Shape because its destructor is virtual.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Runtime dispatch requires a virtual base function and access through a base reference or pointer to an unsliced object.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Runtime dispatch requires a virtual base function and access through a base reference or pointer to an unsliced object.

examples.title: Virtual dispatch and abstract interfaces: boundary case
examples.situation: A virtual call from the base constructor does not reach a derived override whose subobject is not yet constructed.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A virtual call from the base constructor does not reach a derived override whose subobject is not yet constructed.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Calls during construction and destruction dispatch only within the active construction level, default arguments are statically selected, and object slicing removes derived state.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Any same-named derived function is dynamically selected even when the base function is not virtual or the object was copied by value.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and trace runtime dispatch through a small abstract interface.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Any same-named derived function is dynamically selected even when the base function is not virtual or the object was copied by value.
misconceptions.correction: Runtime dispatch requires a virtual base function and access through a base reference or pointer to an unsliced object.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: Any same-named derived function is dynamically selected even when the base function is not virtual or the object was copied by value.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Virtual dispatch and abstract interfaces: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Calls during construction and destruction dispatch only within the active construction level, default arguments are statically selected, and object slicing removes derived state.
